The main commercial industries are those connected with the rice and timber trade. The indigenous manufactures of the country produce little beyond what is required for home consumption. Silk, lacquered ware, and gold and silver work, are among the most justly-admired of Burmese manufactures. The characteristics of Burmese art are vigour and novelty of design, but want of delicacy, and finish in execution.

The dynasty which till recently reigned in Upper Burma was founded in the year 1752 A.D., by Alaungpaya, who, in early life a hunter, then a dacoit leader, established himself on the throne and extended his authority over the whole of Burma, including Arakan, Pegu, and Tenasserim, to the sea and the borders of Siam. The early relations of the House of Alaungpaya with the Indian Government are matters of history. In the reign of Bagyidawpaya, the seventh king and fourth in descent from Alaungpaya, occurred the first war between

England and Burma, terminated in 1825 by the treaty of Yandabo, whereby the provinces of Arakan and Tenasserim were ceded to the British. In the reign of Bagyidawpaya's nephew, Pagan Min, occurred the second Burmese war, at the conclusion of which, in December, 1852, Lord Dalhousie by proclamation annexed to the Indian Empire the province of Pegu, including the deltas of the Irawadi and the Sittaung, and the valleys of those rivers as far north as the parallel of latitude six miles north of the fort at Myede. Ten years later the province was organised, and a Chief Commissioner appointed. The territory which remained to the Burmese king was the country now known as Upper Burma. The pacification of the newly-acquired province of Pegu was a work of much labour, and was not satisfactorily completed till ten years after the annexation.

Almost immediately after the end of the second war, Pagan Min was deposed by his brother, thenceforward known as Mindon Min, or King Mindon. In 1867, a treaty was concluded at Mandalay between the British and Burmese governments, providing for the mutual extradition of criminals, the free intercourse of traders, and the establishment of permanent diplomatic relations between the two countries. So long as Mindon Min lived, though he clung to the obsolete ceremonials to which he was accustomed, and thus debarred the British resident at Mandalay in his later years from access to his presence, there was no breach in the friendship between England and Burma.

In October, 1878, Mindon Min died, and was succeeded by his son, the Thebaw prince. The cruelties which marked the accession of the new monarch attracted more notice than on previous similar occasions. Indignation among Englishmen at the state of affairs in Mandalay, and resentment in the minds of the courtiers of the king on account of the attitude of the resident, combined to render imminent a breach of the friendly relations between the two countries. The immediate apprehension of war passed away; but throughout the year the tension continued, and in October, 1879, in view of the passive unfriendliness of the Burmese government, and the unsatisfactory nature of the position of the British resident in Mandalay, the government of India withdrew their representative from the Burmese court, and, as long as the native government continued to exist, no fresh agent was appointed.

These occurrences were sinister omens for the reign of King Thebaw. Nor did the progress of events afford prospect of the re-settlement of the relations between the Indian and Burmese governments. The actions of the latter soon gave abundant reason for complaint. British subjects, travellers and traders from Lower Burma, were subjected to insult and violence by local officials in Upper Burma, and representations to the central administration were often fruitless in obtaining redress. In contravention of the express terms of the treaty of 1867, monopolies were created to the detriment of the trade of both England and Burma. Owing to the weakness and corruption of the Burmese government, society became thoroughly disorganised, and the elements of disorder on our frontier became a standing menace to the peace of the British provinces. At the same time, the

Burmese government showed a marked and persistent anxiety to enter into alliances with foreign powers, in such a manner and to such an extent as to give ground for apprehension that grave political trouble might hereafter result. While the Indian government was unrepresented at Mandalay, representatives of Italy and France were welcomed, and two separate embassies were sent to Europe, one under the guise of a merely commercial mission, for the purpose of contracting new and, if possible, close alliances with sundry European powers. Such was the state of affairs at the beginning of 1885. Early in that year, the Burmese government found or made reason for complaint against the Bombay-Burma Trading Corporation, a company of merchants, chiefly British subjects, who had extensive dealings in Upper Burma, and, without giving them reasonable opportunity of being heard, condemned them to pay a fine amounting to £230,000. Acting under the orders of the supreme government, the chief commissioner protested against this arbitrary act of the Burmese authorities, and invited them to stay proceedings against the corporation, and to refer the matter to an arbitrator appointed by His Excellency the Viceroy. The reply of the Burmese foreign minister was that on no account should proceedings against the corporation be suspended. In view of this decisive and discourteous refusal to accept the mediation of the British government in a case where the interests of British subjects were largely involved—and bearing in mind the accumulated causes of dissatisfaction with the existing state of things—the government of India decided once for all to adjust the relations between the two countries. The chief commissioner was therefore instructed to send to the King of Burma an ultimatum requiring him to suspend action in the execution of the decree against the corporation, to receive at Mandalay an envoy from the Viceroy with a view to the settlement of the matter at issue, and, hereafter, to permit the residence at the capital of an agent of the Indian government, who should be received and treated with the respect due to the government which he represented. It was further intimated that the Burmese government would in future be required to regulate the external relations of the country in accordance with the advice of the government of India, and to afford facilities for opening up British trade with China. On the 9th November, a reply, amounting to an unconditional refusal of the terms, was received in Rangoon. On the 14th an expeditionary force crossed the frontier. On the 17th, Minhla, on the 23rd, Pagan, and on the 25th, Myingyan, were successively occupied. Except at Minhla, scarcely any resistance was encountered. Before the expeditionary force reached Ava, an envoy from the Burmese court arrived, and, after some negotiation, the unconditional surrender of the capital and of the royal family was arranged. On the 26th and 27th November, the forts at Ava and Sagaing were surrendered; and, on the 28th, General Prendergast and his force occupied Mandalay. The king and his two queens, with their mother, were at once sent down to Rangoon, whence the former were afterwards transferred to India, the latter to Tavoy. Simultaneously with the advance on Mandalay, operations

were undertaken with success on the Thayetmyo and Toungoo frontiers.

Immediately after the occupation of Mandalay a provisional administration was constituted. All the members of the Hlutdaw, or great Council of State, professed themselves willing to continue to take part in the government. They were therefore retained in office, under the guidance of Colonel (afterwards Sir E. B.) Sladen, and the control of General (now Sir Harry) Prendergast. Theoretically there was to be no breach of continuity. The state council was to continue to discharge all its functions, and all civil officers, whether British or Burmese, were to work under its direction. The council began by issuing proclamations to this effect, and ordering Burmese officials to continue in the regular performance of their duties.

On the 1st January, 1886, by the Viceroy's proclamation, Upper Burma was declared to be part of Her Majesty's dominions, and placed under the direct administration of the governor-general.

The military occupation of Mandalay had been effected without the smallest difficulty, and in a manner which reflected credit on all concerned. To this, as was foreseen, succeeded a period of disorder and of straggling warfare which assumed dimensions somewhat, though not materially, exceeding what was anticipated. The same difficulties, however, were encountered upon the annexation of Lower Burma, and were not finally overcome for a period of eight years after the occupation of that province, although the general pacification of the country may be said to have been effected at the end of the third year. Before the rainy season of 1883, all the districts of Upper Burma, except two, namely, Sagaing and Minbu, had been pacified; and by the middle of 1889 all the large gangs of rebels that had given trouble in the plains were completely broken up and the remnants of once powerful bands had taken refuge in the inaccessible broken tracts, which form a marked feature of the upper country.

One of the most urgent wants of Burma is land communication. There is abundance of waterway, however, throughout the delta all the year round, but the Sittang valley has no such advantages. The railway to Mandalay from Toungoo (the then terminus of the Sittang Valley line), for which a preliminary survey was sanctioned in November, 1886, was opened for traffic in March, 1889. Since the railway was opened the districts through which it runs have been the quietest in Upper Burma. The traffic on the line is already considerable. Large outlay has been incurred and still continues upon barracks, hospitals, roads, and necessary civil buildings.

NATIVE STATES.

That the territories of the Native States of India occupy no small proportion of the entire Indian peninsula and contain over one-fifth of the whole population of the country will be seen from the statistical table printed at page 166.

Many of the Feudatory States are governed almost independently by their own rulers, but they are all subject in a greater or less degree to supervision and guidance at the hands of the British Government. They may be divided into two main heads: (1) States holding direct relations with the Governor-General in Council; and (2) States holding relations primarily with the several Local Governments of India.

STATES UNDER THE DIRECT SUPERVISION OF THE GOVERNOR-GENERAL IN COUNCIL.

1. Hyderabad.—The Hyderabad State is roughly co-extensive with the Deccan or central plateau of Southern India and takes its name from its capital city. With the exception of the assigned districts known as Berar, a tract of some 17,000 square miles, the whole of this province is governed by the Nizam. The total area including Berar is about 98,000 square miles, and the population is estimated to be about 12½ millions, one-tenth being Muhammadans. The ruling dynasty is of Turkoman origin, the name of the first Sovereign being Chin Killich Khan, also known as Asaf Jah, who was appointed to be Viceroy of the Deccan with the title of Nizam-ul-Mulk in the year 1713. During the dissensions in the Empire subsequent to the death of Aurungzeb, Asaf Jah succeeded in asserting his own independence. The Assigned Districts of Berar are administered by British officers in trust for the Nizam, and the Government maintains out of their revenues an auxiliary force of four cavalry regiments, four field batteries of artillery, and six regiments of infantry. The raison d'être of this force was primarily that it might be of use to the Nizam, but it is available also under treaty for general service with British troops when required. The surplus revenue derived from Berar, after paying for the cost of the administration, is paid to the Nizam's Government, and now amounts to nearly 20 lakhs of rupees per annum.

2. Mysore.—The Mysore State is situated in Southern India between 11° 40' and 15° N. lat., and between 74° 40' and 78° 30' E. long., and is surrounded on all sides by British territory. Its total area is 24,723 square miles, and it contains a population of nearly 5 millions. The inhabitants are almost exclusively Hindus, who constitute more than 94 per cent. of the whole population. In early times Mysore was the principal seat of the Jains, who have left many interesting memorials of their occupation. The State has always been under Hindu rulers except during the short interval caused by the usurpation of power during the 18th century by Haidar Ali and his son, Tippu Sultan. After the defeat of the latter, at the siege of Seringapatam in 1799, A.D., the English restored a representative of the ancient line in the person of Krishna Raj, son of Chama Raj of Arakotara. His subsequent misrule, when admitted to power on attaining his majority, led to the resumption of the administrative control of the province by the British Government in the year 1831. The government, however, continued to be carried on in the name of the Native Prince, and at his death an adopted son was recognised as successor. This Chief was duly installed on coming of age in 1881. The British Chief Commissioner thereupon handed over office to the Native Diwan, and a

Political Resident was appointed to represent British interests.

3. Baroda.—This State includes all the territories of his Highness the Gaekwar. Its total area is 8,570 square miles, and it contains a population of 2,414,200 souls, the majority of whom (over 89 per cent.) are Hindus. The province of Guzerat was at one time included in the Mogal Empire, but, in the early part of the seventeenth century, the Marhattas, under the leadership of Damaji Gaekwar, and afterwards of his son, Pilaji, succeeded in wresting all power out of the hands of the Mogal officers. From that time Baroda has remained continuously under the sway of the Gaekwar family, who held it in the first instance under the Mahratta Peshwa subject to a tributary payment, but afterwards threw off their allegiance and became feudatory to the British Government under the guarantee of a treaty executed in the year 1817. The British Government is represented at the Court of the Baroda State by a Political Officer, called the Governor-General's Agent.

4. Kashmir and Jammu.—This Native State includes, in addition to the district of Kashmir proper, Jammu, Poonch, and the Governorships of Ladakh and Gilgit. The area is estimated to be 80,900 square miles. The population, according to the preliminary totals of the census of 1891, amounts to over 2,500,000. Kashmir became part of the Mogal Empire in 1586 A.D., but it was afterwards subjugated both by the Afghans and the Sikhs. At the time of the Sikh War the State was under the government of Gulab Singh, a Sikh officer, who ruled as a feudatory of the Maharaja of the Punjab. After the victory of the British forces in 1846, the possession of the kingdom was confirmed to Gulab Singh, under certain specified conditions of alliance and subordinate co-operation, by a separate treaty executed at Amritsar in the same year. A Political Resident is permanently stationed with the head-quarters of the Kashmir State, and represents the authority of the Governor-General; without his concurrence no changes of importance can be carried through by the State Council, of which the Maharaja is president. The ruling family is Hindu, but the greater number of the inhabitants of the country, considerably more than half, are Muhammadans, and chiefly of the Sunni sect.

5. Kalat.—The Khan of Kalat is head of the Baluchistan tribal chiefs whose territories are comprised under the following divisions:—Jhalawan, Sarawan, Makran, Las, and Kachh Gandava. These districts form what may be termed Kalati Baluchistan, and occupy an area of about 80,000 square miles. The inhabitants of the country are either Brahuis or Baluchis, both being Muhammadans of the Sunni sect. The country is sparsely populated, the total number being roughly estimated at 350,000 persons only. It derives its chief importance from its position with regard to Afghanistan on the North-Western Frontier of British India. The Khan of Kalat is under a treaty obligation of subordinate co-operation with the British Government. The Governor-General's Agent in Baluchistan conducts the relations between the Government of India and the Khan, and exercises a general political supervision over the district.

6. Rajputana Agency.—Rajputana is the name of a great territorial circle, situated between 23°

and 30° N. lat., and between 69° 30' and 78° 15' E. long. Within these limits are twenty Native States, having each its own autonomy and separate Chief, together with the British district of Ajmere-Merwara. Of the Native States, 17 are Rajput, 2 are Jat, Bhartpur and Dholpur, and 1 only is Muhammadan, Tonk. Their combined area is about 130,000 square miles, and they contain a population of over 12 millions. The chief administrative control of the British District

is vested ex officio in the Political Officer, who holds the post of Governor-General's Agent for the supervision of the relations between the several Native States of Rajputana and the Government of India.

The following table, compiled chiefly from the returns taken in 1881, gives a detailed statement of the estimated area and population and the approximate revenues of the States in the Rajputana Agency:—

Name. Area

miles.		 Population. Revenue.

Banswara 1,500 152,045 20,000

Dungarpur 1,000 153,381 20,000
Jaipur 14,465 2,534,357 534,000
Jaisalmir 16,447 108,143 21,000
Jhalawar 2,694 340,488 213,000

7. Central India Agency.—Central India is the name given to the country occupied by the Native States grouped together under the supervision of the Political Officer in charge of the Central India Agency. These States lie between 21° 24' and 26° 32' N. lat., and between 74° 0' and 83° 0' E. long. The British Districts of Jhansi and Lalitpur divide the Agency into two main divisions—Native Bundelkhand and Baghelkhand lying to the east, and Central India proper to the west. The total area covered is not less than 75,000 square miles, and the population amounts to over 9 millions. The great majority of the people are Hindus. The principal States

are 6 in Number—Gwalior, Indore, Bhopal, Rewa, Dhar, and Jaora, of which two, Bhopal and Jaora, are Muhammadan, and the rest are Hindu. Besides these there are a multitude of petty States held by their rulers under the immediate guarantee of the British Government, but having feudal relations with one or other of the larger States. The total number of States amounts to 69, and the petty Chieftains to 79—total, 148.

The list below gives the approximate size, population, and revenue of the six principal States above detailed:—

Name. Area. Population. Revenue.
₹
Gwalior 29,046 2,963,952 1,120,000
Indore 8,400 1,054,237 750,000
Bhopal 6,873 954,901 365,000
Rewa... 10,083 1,305,124 150,000
Dhar... 1,740 149,244 76,312
Jaora... 581 108,434 100,000

STATES UNDER THE LOCAL GOVERNMENTS AND ADMINISTRATIONS.

1. Under the Government of Madras.—The Madras Presidency includes five Native States covering an area of 9,638 square miles, with a population of 3,344,349 souls. Revenue, ₹921,300.

Of these States, Travancore and Cochin represent ancient Hindu dynasties. Pudukota is the inheritance of a Chieftain called Tondiman. Banganapalli and Sandur, two petty States, of which the first is ruled by a Nawab, lie in the centre of two British districts. The principal States are:—

Name. Area. Population. Revenue
Cochin ... .. 1,361 600,278 £
161,800
Travancore ... .. 6,730 2,401,158 666,300

2. Under the Government of Bombay.—The Native States in this Presidency number about 370. Area, 71,935 square miles. Population, 6,976,109. Revenue, £2,996,000.

The characteristic feature of the Bombay States is the excessive number of petty principalities, governed mostly by Rajput and Bhil Chieftains. The Peninsula of Kathiawar alone contains no less than 188 separate States. The recognition of these innumerable jurisdictions is due partly

to the circumstance that the early Bombay Administrators were induced to treat the de facto exercise of civil and criminal jurisdiction by a landholder as carrying with it a quasi-sovereign status, and partly to the fact that some of the minor States have at various times been partitioned amongst members of the ruling families.

The list below gives details of the area, population and revenue of the more important States:—

Name. Area. Population. Revenue.
Bhaunagar ... .. 2,860 400,323 £
260,000
Dhrangadra ... .. 1,156 99,686 60,000
Gondal ... .. 1,024 135,604 100,000
Idar ... .. 2,500 258,429 55,200
Junagarh ... .. 3,279 387,499 220,000
Khairpur ... .. 6,109 129,153 63,000
Kolhapur ... .. 2,816 800,189 312,500
Kutch... .. 6,500 512,084 187,000
Morvi... .. 821 89,964 91,000
Nawanagar ... .. 3,791 316,147 218,400
Palanpur ... .. 3,150 234,402 50,000
Rajpipla ... .. 1,514 114,756 60,000

3. Under the Government of Bengal there are twenty-nine States. Area, 39,234 square miles. Population, 2,853,405. Revenue, £245,900.

These States may be divided into two groups: the first consisting of the petty States attached to the Orissa and Chota Nagpur Divisions, 26 in number; and the other comprising the States of Kuch Behar and Sikkim to the north, and Hill Tipperah to the east of Bengal. The inhabitants

of the first group are hillmen of Kolarian or Dravidian origin, and their condition is still very primitive. Kuch Behar is inhabited by an Indo-Chinese people, the Koch. The population of Sikkim consists of Lepchas, Bhutias and Limbus. Hill Tipperah is the home of a race, partly Indo-Burmese, and partly drawn from the Eastern Himalayas.

The principal States are:—

Name. Area. Population. Revenue.
Kuch Behar... .. 1,307 602,624 £
132,000
Hill Tipperah ... .. 4,086 95,637 24,400
Sikkim ... .. 2,600 8,000 1,900

4. Under the Government of the N.-W.-P. and Oudh, the native territory comprises the following two States, the first being ruled by a

Muhammadan, and the second by a Hindu dynasty.

Name. Area. Population. Revenue.
Rampur ... .. 945 541,914 £
169,400
Tehri (Garhwal) ... .. 1,180 199,836 14,200

Rampur is nearly equally divided between Muhammadans and Hindus. The inhabitants of Tehri are chiefly Hindus.

5. Under the Government of the Punjab there are thirty-four States, varying considerably in size and importance. Area, 35,817 square miles. Population, 3,861,683. Revenue, £1,119,100.

The Punjab States may be grouped under three main classes. The Hill States, 23 in number, lying among the Punjab Himalayas and held by some of the most ancient Rajput families in all India. Along the western half of

the southern border lies the Muhammadan State of Bahawalpur. The remaining States, including the Sikh Principalities of Patiala, Jind, Nabha, Kapurthala, Faridkot and Kalsia, and the Muhammadan Chiefships of Maler Kotla, Pataudi, Loharu and Dujana, lie east of Lahore, and, with insignificant exceptions, occupy the centre of the eastern plains of the province.

The list below gives details of the area, population, and revenue of the more important States:—

Name. Area. Population. Revenue.
Bahawalpur... 15,000 573,494 £160,000
Chamba ... 3,180 115,773 23,000
Faridkot ... 612 97,034 30,000
Jind ... 1,232 249,862 60,000

Maler Kotla ... 164 71,051 28,000
Mandi ... 1,000 147,017 36,000
Nabha ... 928 261,824 70,000
Patiala ... 5,887 1,467,433 493,346
Sarmur (Nahan) ... 1,077 112,371 21,000

6. Under the Chief Commissioner of the Central Provinces are fifteen States. None of them is of any size or importance. Their total area is 28,834 square miles, with a population of 1,709,720 persons. Revenue, £77,700. The inhabitants are chiefly Hindus and aborigines.

there is only one State—Manipur. Its area is 8,000 square miles, and it has a population of 220,000, principally Hindus. Revenue, £9,000.

8. Under the Chief Commissioner of Burma there are some Shan States and other petty Chiefships, the population and revenue of which are at present unknown.

7. Under the Chief Commissioner of Assam

NOTE.—The statistics of population given in the foregoing note are taken for the most part from the census of 1881. The detailed figures for 1891 are not yet available.

LIST OF PARLIAMENTARY PAPERS RELATING TO INDIA ISSUED DURING THE
SESSION OF 1890-91.

H.C. 250. Moral and Material Progress Report, 1889-90.
C. 6409. Report on Railways in India, 1890-91.
C. 6501. Report on Sanitary Measures in India, 1889-90.
C. 6502. Statistical Abstract, 1889-90.
H.C. 243. Home Accounts, 1889-90.
H.C. 242. Estimate of Revenue and Expenditure, 1890-91.
H.C. 225. Financial Statement, 1891-92.
C. 6454. Accounts and Estimates, 1891-92 : Explanatory Memorandum.
H.C. 27. Loans raised in England to 30th September, 1890.
H.C. 178. Loans raised in England to 31st March, 1891.
H.C. 66. Loans raised in India to 30th September, 1890.
H.C. 296. Loans raised in India to 31st March, 1891.
C. 6341. Trade of British India, 1885-86 to 1889-90.
H.C. 157. Indigo Cultivation in Bengal : Correspondence.
H.C. 384. Opium, Article on : From the Dictionary of Economic Products.
H.C. 86. Report of the Indian Factories Commission.
H.C. 194. Indian Factory Act, 1891.
H.C. 120. Indian Factory Act : Correspondence.
H.C. 224. Indian Factory Act : Further Correspondence.
C. 6353. Manipur, Nos. 1 and 2 : Correspondence.
C. 6356. Manipur, No. 3 : Correspondence.
C. 6410. Manipur, No. 4 : Correspondence.
H.C. 392. Manipur, No. 5 : Further Correspondence.
C. 6548. Ditto ditto.
H.C. 258. Manipur : Papers relating to.
H.C. 278. Manipur Succession.
C. 6208. Sikkim and Tibet : Convention between Great Britain and China.
C. 6526. Orakzai and Black Mountain Expedition.
H.C. 156. Cambay Disturbances : Correspondence.
H.C. 85. Scarcity in Kumaon and Garhwal.
H.C. 127. India Council : List of Members, &c.

LIST OF THE MORE IMPORTANT INDIAN GOVERNMENT PUBLICATIONS
ISSUED DURING 1891.

THE FOLLOWING HAVE BEEN ADDED TO THE STOCK OF BOOKS ON SALE AT THE INDIA OFFICE.

Behar Peasant Life. By G. A. Grierson. (10s.) Memoir on the Indian Surveys, 1875-90. By C. E. D. Black. (7s. 6d.)
Dictionary of Indian Economic Products. Vols. 3 to 5. (Subscription for the Series, 3l. 3s.) Scientific Memoirs. By Indian Medical Officers. Part VI. (6s.)
Finance and Revenue Accounts of the Government of India, 1889-90. (2s. 6d.) Scientific Results of the Second Yarkand Mission. Coleoptera, (3s.) Aves, (20s.) Introduction and Map, (1s. 6d.)
Manual of Hydraulics. By Captain H. D. Love, R.E. (5s.)

IN ADDITION TO THE USUAL PROVINCIAL AND DEPARTMENTAL REPORTS, THE FOLLOWING WORKS OF GENERAL INTEREST HAVE BEEN PUBLISHED IN INDIA.

Prices and Wages in India, 8th issue. (R. 1-8.) Ethnographical Handbook for the North-Western Provinces and Oudh. By W. Crooke, B.A. (Rs. 2-8.)
Lighthouses and Lightvessels in British India, 11th issue. (R. 1.) Monumental Antiquities and Inscriptions in the North-Western Provinces and Oudh. By A. Führer, Ph. D. (Rs. 10.)
Western Thibet; A Practical Dictionary of the Language and Customs of the Ladak Wazarat. By Capt. H. Ramsay. (Rs. 2.) Karnataka Sabdanusanam. Edited by B. L. Rice, C.I.E. (Rs. 10.)
Epigraphia Indica, Parts 6 & 7. (Rs. 3 each.) Inscriptions at Sravana Belgola (Mysore.) (Rs. 12.)
Indigo. By Dr. G. Watt, C.I.E. (R. 1.) Notes and Statistics of Burmah. 2nd edition. (Rs. 2-10.)
Central Provinces Code, 1891. (Rs. 5.) Report on Coalfields in N. Shan States. By Dr. F. Noetling. (8a.)
Indian Evidence Act, 1882, as amended to May, 1891. (R. 1.) Report on Tin Mining in Mergui District. By T. W. H. Hughes. (8a.)
Records of the Geological Survey. Vol. 23, Part IV; vol. 24, Parts I to III. (R. 1 per part, or Rs. 2 per year of 4 parts.) Report on Tin Mining in Perak and Burma. (R. 1.)
Records of the Geological Survey. Index to vols. 1 to 20. Report on Oil Fields of Twingaung and Bemé. (R. 1-3.)
Palæontologia Indica, Series 13, vol. 4, Part II. (Rs. 2.)
Tribes and Castes of Bengal. By H. H. Risley.

THE FOLLOWING ARE THE PRINCIPAL MAPS ADDED TO THE SALE STOCK AT THE INDIA OFFICE.

(Except where otherwise stated, the maps are on one sheet. The figures in italics denote the number of miles to the inch.)

General Maps.—India, 32 (six sheets, 16s.); India, 64 (four sheets, 12s.); India, 64 (two sheets, 6s.); India, shewing telegraphs, 32 (six sheets, 16s.); India, shewing railways, 32 (six sheets, 16s.); India, shewing railways, with hills, 48 (four sheets, 12s.); India, shewing railways, with hills, 64 (two sheets, 8s.); India, shewing railway gauges, 96 (4s.); India, shewing meteorological stations, 128 (2s. 6d.); Bombay Presidency, 32 (3s. 6d.); Madras Presidency, 32 (4s.); N.W. Provinces and Oudh, 32 (1s. 6d.); Punjab, 16 (four sheets, 10s.); Punjab, 32 (2s.); Punjab (skeleton) 32 (1s. 6d.); Afghanistan, 24 (four sheets, 7s.); Mysore (preliminary), 16 (1s. 3d.).

Divisional and District Maps.—Ballia District, 2 (two sheets, 5s.); Bijnor District, 2 (four sheets, 5s.); Bilaspur District, 2 (two sheets, 5s.); Cachar District, 4 (2s. 6d.); Chittagong District, 4 (two sheets, 4s.); Chittagong Division, 8 (2s.); Dacca Division, 8 (3s. 6d.); Dar-

jeeling District, 4 (2s.); Darrang District, 4 (2s. 6d.); Garo Hills District, 2 (four sheets, 4s.); Ghazipur District, 2 (4s.); Gya District, 4 (2s. 6d.); Jalpaiguri and Kuch Behar District, 4 (2s. 6d.); Jaunpur District, 2 (5s.); Lakimpur District, 4 (2s. 6d.); Presidency Division, 8 (2s. 6d.); Rungpore District, 4 (2s. 6d.); Singhbhoom District, 4 (2s. 6d.).

Indian Atlas Sheets.—4, Nos. 15 (4s.), 13 N.W., 15 N.W., 21 S.W., 31 S.E., 50 S.E., 60 N.W., 76 S.E. (1s. 6d. each).

Miscellaneous.—South Eastern Frontier (preliminary), 8, sheets 1 and 3A (per sheet 3s. 6d.); North Eastern Frontier (preliminary), 8, sheet 15 (3s. 6d.); Chin Hills (preliminary), 4 (3s. 6d.); Anglo-Siamese Boundary Commission Map (preliminary), 12 (2s. 6d.); Andaman Islands, 4 (1s.); Benares City and Cantonment, 6 (four sheets, 3s. 6d.); Black Mountain District, 2 (2s.); Baluchistan and Sind Frontier (skeleton), 32, (2s.).

NOTE.—Full lists of the publications issued by the various Governments in India may be obtained on application to the Registrar and Superintendent of Records, India Office, or to any of the following

AGENTS TO THE INDIA OFFICE FOR THE SALE OF GOVERNMENT PUBLICATIONS.

For Books and Maps.

Mr. E. A. ARNOLD, 37, Bedford Street, Strand, W.C.
Messrs. A. CONSTABLE and Co., 14, Parliament Street, S.W.
Messrs. LUZAC and Co., 46, Great Russell Street, W.C.
Messrs. KEGAN PAUL, TRENCH, TRÜBNER and

Co., Paternoster House, Charing Cross Road, W.C.

Mr. B. QUARITCH, 15, Piccadilly, W.

For Maps Only.

Mr. E. STANFORD, 26, Cockspur Street, Charing Cross, S.W.
Messrs. H. S. KING and Co., 65, Cornhill, E.C.

THE INDIA OFFICE LIST

RECORD OF THE PUBLIC SERVICES OF OFFICERS AND OTHERS

DESCRIPTION OF SERVICE

RECORD

OF THE

PUBLIC SERVICES OF OFFICERS AND OTHERS.

[The following List includes Officers of the Home Establishment of the Secretary of State, Officers of the Covenanted Civil Service, and those appointed under the Native Civil Service Rules, other Officers in Civil Employment in India whose SUBSTANTIVE pay is at least Rs. 1,000 a month, and Officers of those classes who have retired since 1886. It has not been found practicable to include more than a limited number of the Officers still surviving who retired before 1886.]

THE INDIA OFFICE LIST.

RECORD OF THE PUBLIC SERVICES OF OFFICERS AND OTHERS.

DESCRIPTION OF SERVICES.

ABBOTT, HENRY BYAM, Lieutenant-Colonel I.S.C., Political Department, Government of India.—First commission, June, 1861; arrived in India, Nov., 1861; served with the 100th foot; served as assistant resident, Aden, 1865 to 1869; from Oct., 1869, to Feb., 1874, served as assistant agent, Rajputana, and acted as magistrate of Abu, and assistant general superintendent, Thagi and Dakaiti department, 1872-73; officiating settlement officer, Ulwar, 1874-75; officiating political agent, Eastern States, Rajputana, 1875; political assistant, Manpura, and deputy Bheel agent, 1875-76; on special duty at Jhallawar, 1876-77; superintendent of Jhallawar, from March, 1876; graded as additional political agent, 1st class, Dec., 1888; officiating resident at Gwalior, July, 1890; and at Meywar, Oct., 1890; political agent, Ulwar, Jan., 1891; officiating resident, Jeypur, May, 1891.

ABDUL GHAFUR KHAN OF ZAIDA, Punjab Commission.—Appointed probationer under native civil service rules, 14th Feb., 1889.

ABU TURAB KHAN, AGHA, N.W. Provinces, Native C.S.—Appointed by the Government of India a probationary assistant magistrate and collector under the statutory civil service rules, 16th April, 1880.

ACKLOM, ROBERT EVATT, Police Department, Government of India.—Appointed assistant district superintendent of police, 18th April, 1867, and district superintendent, June, 1872; appointed superintendent Rajputana-Malwa railway police, May, 1883.

ACWORTH, HARRY ARBUTHNOT, Barrister-at-Law, Bombay C.S.—Educated at Brighton College; appointed to the Bombay civil service after open competitive examination of 1868; arrived in India, 31st Oct., 1870, and served as assistant collector and magistrate, acting collector of stamp revenue and political agent, and acting collector and magistrate; acting under secretary to government of Bombay, revenue, financial, and general departments, Oct., 1879; acting collector of Bombay and superintendent of stamps and stationery, March, 1881; from April, 1881, served as assistant collector, deputy collector, and acting junior collector of salt revenue; member of the mixed commission at Goa, Dec., 1884; deputy municipal commissioner, July, 1885; deputy collector of salt revenue, Aug., 1885; acting junior collector and magistrate, Aug., 1887; municipal commissioner for city of Bombay, April, 1890; editor of the Bombay Revenue Handbook, 3rd edition.

ADAMS, GEORGE, Bengal C.S. (North-Western Provinces).—Educated at Christ's Hospital; appointed to the Bengal civil service after open competitive examination of 1860; arrived in India, 30th Dec., 1861; from April, 1863, served in the N.W. Provinces as assistant magistrate and collector; deputy commissioner, Lalitpur, in the Jhansi commission, April, 1877; officiating commissioner, Jhansi, in 1878 and 1880; magistrate and collector, Nov., 1883; officiating commissioner, Sitapur, July, 1888; commissioner in Oudh, Nov., 1889; commissioner and governor-general's agent, Sitapur, Aug., 1890.

ADAMSON, CHARLES HENRY ELLISON, C.I.E., Major, I.S.C., Burma Commission.—First commission, royal artillery, Jan., 1868; served with 5th brigade at Kirkee and Rangoon till 1870; admitted to the Madras staff corps, 1870; joined the Burma commission, Dec., 1871, and served as assistant magistrate, assistant commissioner, and officiating magistrate, and president of municipal committee, Rangoon; deputy commissioner, June, 1885; C.I.E., May, 1889.

ADAMSON, HARVEY, M.A., Bengal C.S. (Burma).—Educated at the Gymnasium, Old Aberdeen, and King's College, Aberdeen; appointed to the Bengal civil service after open competitive examination of 1875; arrived at Rangoon, 17th Dec., 1877; served as assistant commissioner, officiating deputy commissioner, and settlement officer; 1st grade settlement officer, June, 1886; deputy commissioner, 4th grade, April, 1888; 3rd grade, Dec., 1888; 2nd grade, Nov., 1890.

ADDIS, DAVID FOULIS, M.A., Bengal C.S. (North-Western Provinces).—Educated at the Universities of Glasgow and Edinburgh; appointed to the Bengal civil service after open competitive examination of 1870; arrived in India, 14th Nov., 1872, and served in the N.W. Provinces as assistant magistrate and collector, joint magistrate, assistant settlement officer, and officiating magistrate and collector; joint magistrate, 2nd grade, Dec., 1883; 1st grade, August, 1887; deputy commissioner, Jan., 1890; magistrate and collector, July, 1890.

AGNEW, PATRICK DALREAGLE, Bengal C.S. (Punjab).—Educated at Bedford Grammar School and Balliol College, Oxford; appointed to the Bengal civil service after open competition of 1887; arrived in India 1st Dec., 1889, and served in the Punjab as assistant commissioner.

AGNEW, WILLIAM FISCHER, Barrister-at-Law, Judicial Department, Burma.—Reporter

for Indian law reports, high court, Calcutta, from Feb., 1877; officiated as editor in 1879, 1882, and 1883; law lecturer, Presidency College, 1879; advocate, high court, Calcutta, 1879; recorder of Rangoon, March, 1884; officiating judge, high court, Calcutta in 1885 and 1886, author of 'Law of Patent,' 'The Statute of Frauds,' 'The Law of Trusts in India,' 'The Criminal Procedure Code,' and 'Index to Indian Acts.'

AHSANUDDIN AHMAD, MOULVIE, Barrister-at-Law, Bengal C.S.—Appointed assistant magistrate and collector on probation under the native civil service rules, Dec., 1881; confirmed in the statutory civil service, June, 1887.

AIKMAN, ROBERT SMITH, M.A., Bengal C.S. (North-Western Provinces).—Educated at Madras College, St. Andrew's, and St. Andrew's University; appointed to the Bengal civil service after open competitive examination of 1865; arrived in India, 22nd Nov., 1867, and serve in the N.W. Provinces as assistant magistrate and collector, and officiating magistrate and collector, and was transferred temporarily to Bengal on famine relief duty, Feb. to Oct., 1874; promoted to joint magistrate and deputy collector, Jan., 1879; from March 1884 served in the judicial department, and was appointed judge, 3rd grade, Oct., 1886, and 2nd grade, March, 1890.

AINSLIE, WILLIAM, late Bengal C.S.—Appointed to the Bengal civil service from Haileybury College, 1845; arrived in India, 7th Nov., 1845; served in the Bengal revenue and judicial departments in the grades of assistant, and joint magistrate and deputy collector in Cuttack; and was specially employed in 1850-51 and 1851-52 in the suppression of Meriah sacrifices in the Khond districts; magistrate of Runpore, July, 1852; railway commissioner, East Indian railway, Jan., 1856, to March, 1857; magistrate and collector, Bhagulpore, March, 1857; railway commissioner, Eastern Bengal railway, 1858, and subsequently of the South-Eastern railway also; from Aug., 1863, served in the judicial department, and was appointed civil and sessions judge of Patna, Jan., 1864; president, tea commission, Nov., 1867, to April, 1868; officiated as judge of the high court, Calcutta, from Nov., 1870, and was confirmed in that appointment May, 1873; retired, April, 1880.

ATCHISON, SIR CHARLES UMPHERSTON, K.C.S.I., C.I.E., M.A., LL.D., D.C.L., late Bengal C.S.—Educated at the University of Edinburgh; appointed to the Bengal civil service after open competitive examination of 1855; arrived in India, 24th Sept., 1856, and served in the N.W. Provinces as assistant commissioner and magistrate; transferred to Punjab, May, 1857; personal assistant to judicial commissioner, July, 1858; officiating under secretary, government of India, foreign department, Nov., 1859; deputy-commissioner, Punjab, Jan., 1865; officiating commissioner, Lahore, Aug., 1867; officiating secretary to government, Punjab, Nov., 1867; from April, 1868, served in the foreign department, government of India, as officiating secretary, and was confirmed in this appointment, Aug., 1870; on special duty in Thagi and Dakaiti department, Oct., 1877; chief commissioner, British Burma, 31st March, 1878;

provisional member, governor-general's council, July, 1880; lieutenant-governor, Punjab, 4th April, 1882, to April, 1887; member, governor-general's council, 2nd April, 1887, from which date he resigned the civil service; resigned his seat as member of governor-general's council, 1889; has published 'Manual of the Criminal Law of the Punjab,' 1860; 'Treaties, Engagements, and Sanads relating to India and the Neighbouring Countries;' and 'The Native States of India.'

ALCOCK, JOHN BACKHOUSE, Bombay C.S.—Appointed to the Bombay civil service after open competitive examination of 1874; arrived in India, 10th Nov., 1876, and served as assistant collector and magistrate, acting cantonment magistrate, and railway magistrate; from Jan., 1884, served as assistant judge and sessions judge, and as acting judge and sessions judge.

ALEXANDER, ERNEST BRUCE, Bengal C.S. (North-Western Provinces).—Educated at Repton; appointed to the Bengal civil service after open competitive examination of 1869; arrived in India, 1st Nov., 1871, and served in the N.W. Provinces as assistant magistrate and collector; from Nov., 1874, to March, 1881, served as assistant settlement officer and officiating settlement officer in Fatehpur and Moradabad, and was appointed junior secretary to the board of revenue in addition to his other duties in 1877, and was placed on special duty under the orders of the board, Feb., 1879; served subsequently as assistant magistrate, and joint magistrate, and deputy collector, and acted for various periods as small cause judge, and magistrate, and collector; joint magistrate, 1st grade, Sept., 1885; deputy commissioner, June, 1889; magistrate and collector, Oct., 1889; author of 'Gazetteer of Gorakhpur.'

ALEXANDER, JAMES WILLIAM, Public Works Department, Government of India.—Joined the service, June, 1865; assistant engineer, Dec., 1866; assistant secretary to government of India, public works department, Jan., 1868; executive engineer, March, 1870; served in various divisions, and from Nov., 1886, to May, 1887, had charge of the Rohilkhand executive division; 3rd superintending engineer, Feb., 1890; 2nd, June, 1891.

ALEXANDER, MOSES, Store Department, India Office.—Appointed 2nd class clerk, 3rd March, 1888.

ALEXANDER, NATHANIEL STUART, late Bengal C.S.—Appointed from Haileybury College; arrived in India, 10th July, 1858; from Oct., 1859, served in the Bengal revenue and judicial departments, and was appointed magistrate and collector, 1st grade, Jan., 1879; commissioner, Dacca division, Aug., 1884; commissioner of Bhagulpore, Dec., 1885; and of Burdwan, Aug., 1887; retired March, 1890.

ALISON, SIR ARCHIBALD, Bart., G.C.B., General, Member of the council of India.—1st commission ensign 72nd foot, 3rd Nov., 1846; captain, Nov. 1853; major unattached, Dec., 1856; brevet colonel, March, 1867; lieutenant-general, Nov., 1882; served in the Crimean campaign of 1855, and was present at siege of Sevastopol (medal and clasp and Turkish medal); served in the Indian mutiny, and was military secretary to the commander-in-chief, Aug., 1857, to March, 1858 was present at relief

of Lucknow (wounded—arm lost, medal and clasp, brevet of lieutenant-colonel and C.B.); in staff employment adjutant general's department at headquarters, and in the south western district, and at Aldershot, 1862 to 1873; served in the Ashantee war of 1873-4 as brigadier-general of European brigade, and was present at the battle of Amoafu, capture of Bequah, action of Ordashu, and taking of Coomassie; received thanks of both houses of parliament, medal with clasp, and K.C.B.; deputy adjutant general headquarters Ireland, Oct., 1874, to Dec., 1877; commandant staff college, Jan.-April, 1878; deputy quartermaster-general intelligence branch, May, 1878-Aug., 1882; served in the Egyptian expedition of 1882 as major-general in command of troops at Alexandria, and on the Mahmondiyeh canal, and in command of 3rd brigade, and division, at battle of Tel-el-Kebir; received thanks of both houses of parliament, medal with clasp, bronze star, and class Osmanieh; promoted lieutenant-general for distinguished service; commanding division Aldershot, Aug., 1883, to Feb., 1885; and again Oct., 1885; adjutant-general to the forces, Feb.-Sept., 1885; appointed member of the council of India, Jan., 1889.

ALLEN, CHARLES FRANCIS EGERTON, B.A. (Cantab), late Judicial Department, Burma.—Called to the bar (Inner Temple) 1871; admitted advocate of high court, Calcutta, Nov., 1873; law examiner, Calcutta University, Dec., 1874, and again in 1875 and 1876; acting judge of the Calcutta court of small causes, 1875-76; lecturer on English law in the Presidency College, Calcutta, Feb., 1876, and professor of law (temporary) May, 1877; served as government advocate, Rangoon, and acted as recorder of Rangoon for various periods.

ALLEN, CHARLES GEORGE HILLERSDEN, Bengal C.S.—Educated at Haileybury and Balliol College, Oxford; appointed to the Bengal civil service after open competitive examination of 1883; arrived in India, 16th Dec., 1885; assistant magistrate and collector, Jan., 1886.

ALLEN, DAVID BIRD, Bengal C.S.—Educated at Eton, and St. John's College, Cambridge; appointed to the Bengal civil service after open competitive examination of 1871; arrived in India, 5th Nov., 1873; served in revenue and judicial departments, Bengal, as assistant magistrate and collector, assistant to director-general of statistics, and officiating joint magistrate and deputy collector, and under the director of the agricultural department, May, 1885; joint magistrate and deputy collector, Sept., 1887; magistrate and collector, Aug., 1890.

ALLEN, PERCY UNDERHILL, B.A., Bengal C.S. (North-Western Provinces).—Educated at University College School, Cheltenham College, and Magdalen College, Oxford; appointed to the Bengal civil service after open competition of 1885; arrived in India 21st Nov., 1888, and served in the North-Western Provinces as assistant magistrate and collector.

ALLEN, THOMAS TAYLOR, Bengal C.S.—Educated at Queen's College, Cork; appointed to Bengal civil service after open competitive examination of 1859; arrived in India, 7th Feb., 1861; from Nov., 1861, served in the Bengal revenue and judicial departments, and was appointed joint magistrate and deputy collector,

1st grade, Dec., 1867; district and sessions judge, 1st grade, April, 1880; lieutenant-governor's council, Jan., 1881, and again, Jan. to Dec., 1883, and Nov., 1886, and Dec., 1888; superintendent and rememberer of legal affairs, Feb., 1883.

ALLEN, WILLIAM, Bombay C.S.—Educated at King's College School, and St. Catherine's College, Cambridge; appointed to the Bombay civil service after open competitive examination of 1866; arrived in India, 10th Oct., 1868, and served as assistant collector and magistrate, acting railway magistrate, acting 1st class deputy collector in Scinde, acting assistant conservator of forests, and acting collector and magistrate and political agent; 1st assistant collector, Dec., 1882; junior collector and magistrate, Nov., 1889.

ALLISON, HAZLETT, M.D., Surgeon-Major, Madras Medical Department.—Joined the service as surgeon, Sep., 1873; arrived in India, 29th April, 1874, and served on military duty from July, 1874; acted as fort surgeon, Fort St. George, medical inspector of seamen, and professor of anatomy in medical college at various periods; surgeon-major, Sept., 1885; fort surgeon, Fort St. George, with fort and marine duties, and professor of anatomy in the medical college, and medical inspector of seamen, Jan., 1888.

AMBICA CHARAN SEN, M.A., Bengal C.S.—Appointed under the native civil service rules assistant magistrate and collector, Jan., 1884, on probation; on duty under the director of the agricultural department, May, 1885; confirmed in the statutory civil service, Feb., 1886.

AMEER HOSSEIN, SYED, C.I.E., Bengal Judicial Department.—Joined the service as officiating deputy magistrate and deputy collector, 29th Aug., 1864, confirmed 5th Feb., 1867; member of council of lieutenant-governor, April, 1878; officiated as presidency magistrate, Calcutta, for various periods, and was confirmed in that appointment Nov., 1888; member of central examination committee, April, 1886; additional member of governor-general's council, June, 1886; C.I.E. Jan., 1888; coroner of Calcutta, Nov., 1888; officiating presidency magistrate, Nov., 1888; confirmed, Jan., 1889.

AMIR ALI, C.I.E., Bengal Judicial Department.—Appointed judge of the High Court at Calcutta, Jan., 1890.

AMIRTO LAL CHATTERJEE, B.L., Bengal Judicial Department.—From 19th May, 1862, served in the subordinate judicial service, Bengal, and was appointed subordinate judge, 4th grade, and small cause court judge, Moorshedabad, April, 1873; subordinate judge, Nuddea, Jan., 1880; 1st grade subordinate judge, Jan., 1880.

AMOS, ROBERT, Customs Department, Bengal.—Served in the customs department from 4th Dec., 1876, and was appointed assistant collector and head appraiser, Calcutta, July, 1882.

ANDERSON, ALEXANDER, Bengal C.S. (Punjab).—Educated at Gordon's Hospital, New Grammar School, and King's College, Aberdeen; appointed to the Bengal civil service after open competitive examination of 1870; arrived in India, 4th Nov., 1872, and served in the Punjab as assistant commissioner, officiating

deputy commissioner, officiating judicial assistant, and forest settlement officer; on special duty, Jan.-May, 1878, and again, Nov., 1881, in the forest department; assistant commissioner, 2nd grade, Sept., 1882; 1st grade, Feb., 1887; deputy commissioner, March, 1889; on special duty, at Hissar, Nov., 1890.

ANDERSON, FRANCIS CHARLES, Bengal C.S. (Central Provinces).—Educated at Edinburgh Academy, and Repton; appointed to the Bengal civil service after open competitive examination of 1869; arrived in India, 31st Oct., 1871; from Nov., 1871, served in the Central Provinces as assistant commissioner, personal assistant to the chief commissioner, and acting deputy commissioner; assistant secretary to the chief commissioner, Aug., 1881; commissioner of excise, superintendent of stamps, and inspector-general of registration, Feb., 1882; acted as secretary to chief commissioner in Oct., 1882, 1885, and March, 1888; confirmed in that appointment, Jan., 1891; prepared excise manual for the Central Provinces, 1885.

ANDERSON, HENRY AIKEN, Bengal C.S. (Punjab).—Educated at Edinburgh Academy, and Repton Grammar School; appointed to the Bengal civil service after open competitive examination of 1871; arrived in India, 13th Nov., 1873, and served in the Punjab as assistant commissioner, officiating judicial assistant, and officiating deputy commissioner, and officiating under secretary to Government, March, 1880; assistant political officer, Kurram, under the Government of India, Aug., 1880; on special duty, camp of exercise, Delhi, Nov., 1885; officiating district judge, in 1884, 1886, and 1887; deputy commissioner, 3rd grade, May, 1889; officiating divisional judge, April, 1890.

ANDERSON, JAMES, M.A., Barrister-at-Law, late Bengal C.S.—Educated at Edinburgh University; appointed to the Bengal civil service after open competitive examination of 1860; arrived, 14th Nov., 1861; served in the Bengal revenue and judicial departments as assistant magistrate and collector, joint magistrate and deputy collector, and deputy commissioner, and was appointed magistrate and collector, 1st grade, March, 1884; on special duty connected with the Wahabi case, June, 1870; retired, 1888.

ANDERSON, JAMES DRUMMOND, Bengal C.S. (Assam).—Educated at Rugby; appointed to the Bengal civil service after open competitive examination of 1873; arrived in India, 14th Nov., 1875; served in Bengal as assistant magistrate and collector, and officiating joint magistrate and deputy collector; transferred to Assam, Jan., 1880, and served as assistant commissioner and officiating deputy commissioner; assistant secretary to chief commissioner, Jan.-Dec., 1882; assistant commissioner, 1st grade, July, 1886; deputy commissioner, April, 1889; author of 'A Short List of Words of the Hill Tipperah Language,' 1885.

ANDERSON, JOHN ALEXANDER, B.L., Bengal C.S. (Punjab).—Educated at the Grammar School and University of Aberdeen, and was also a student of the Inner Temple; appointed to the Bengal civil service after open competitive examination of 1872; arrived in India, 2nd Dec., 1874, and served in the Punjab as assistant commissioner, officiating judicial assistant, officiating

deputy commissioner, and officiating district judge; officiating divisional judge, March, 1888, and again in 1889 and from April, 1891; district judge, 2nd grade, April, 1889.

ANDERSON, J. W. M., Colonel, late Revenue Survey Department, Bombay.—First commission, Jan., 1855; from Nov., 1863, served in the revenue survey department, Mysore, as assistant superintendent and deputy superintendent; from April, 1881, served as superintendent of the Scinde settlement survey; 1st grade superintendent, Jan., 1885; retired, March, 1888.

ANDERSON, ROBERT HILDEBRAND, Bengal C.S.—Appointed to the Bengal civil service after open competitive examination of 1874; arrived in India, 7th Dec., 1876; served as assistant magistrate and collector, officiating embarkation agent and superintendent of emigration, and officiating joint magistrate and deputy collector; confirmed as joint magistrate and deputy collector, 2nd grade, Aug., 1887, 1st grade, April, 1889; has also acted as district and sessions judge.

ANDERSON, WILLIAM ROBERT LE GEYT, Major, I.S.C.—First commission, 63rd foot, Oct., 1870; admitted to the Bombay staff corps, Oct., 1871; aide-de-camp to Governor of Bombay, 1876; deputy accountant-general, military department, Jan., 1882; military accountant, 2nd class, March, 1890; served in the Egyptian war, 1882 (medal and Khedive's star).

ANDREW, DAVID CLAPHAM, Lieutenant-Colonel, I.S.C., late Survey of India.—Engineer, Bengal Infantry, Dec., 1859; arrived in India, 7th March, 1860; joined the revenue survey department as assistant surveyor, Dec., 1862; deputy superintendent of revenue survey, 3rd grade, Jan., 1878; was in charge of Bassein District, British Burma survey, Nov., 1879, and was transferred to the South Deccan survey, Nov., 1880; 2nd grade deputy superintendent, March, 1886; retired, March, 1889.

ANDREW, JAMES, Madras C.S.—Educated at the Royal School, Armagh, and Kingstown School; appointed to the Madras civil service after open competitive examination of 1877; arrived in India, 17th Nov., 1879, and served in Madras as assistant collector and magistrate, acting sub-collector and joint magistrate, and forest settlement officer; head assistant to collector and magistrate, April, 1888.

ANDREWS, W., Accounts Department, India Office.—Clerk, 2nd class, India office, 30th March, 1876.

ANLEY, GEORGE AUGUSTUS D'AUVERGNE, late Public Works Department, Bengal.—Appointed by the government of India as executive engineer, 4th class, 25th Jan., 1861; served in the Bengal public works department, and temporarily under the municipality of Calcutta; officiating assistant to chief engineer and assistant secretary to the government of Bengal, April, 1876; held charge of office of chief engineer, and secretary to Government of Bengal, Dec., 1878; superintending engineer, 3rd class, March, 1882; 2nd class, March, 1886; retired, Nov., 1888.

ANNESLEY, ROBERT, Major, Judicial Department, N.W. Provinces.—Entered the army in 1855, and served in the Crimean and Abyssinian campaigns; retired in 1869; joined the service, 4th Jan., 1871, as assistant district superin-

tendent of police and officiating cantonment magistrate, and was confirmed in the latter appointment, June, 1877; judge, small cause court, June, 1880; 1st class cantonment magistrate, Oct., 1886.

ANSON, GEORGE WEMYSS, Major, I.S.C., Oudh Commission.—1st commission, 11th Jan., 1867; private secretary and A.D.C. to lieutenant-governor, N.W. Provinces, May, 1871, and Feb., 1875—June, 1878; from April, 1872, served as officiating cantonment magistrate and officiating assistant commissioner; confirmed in latter appointment, Sept., 1875, and in the former, Sept., 1877; agent, Balrampur estate, June, 1886; in addition cantonment magistrate, Aug., 1887; and deputy commissioner, Nov., 1890.

ARBUTHNOT, SIR ALEXANDER JOHN, K.C.S.I., C.I.E., late Madras C.S., member of the Council of India.—Educated at Rugby, and Haileybury College, and appointed to the Madras civil service in 1842; arrived in Madras, Sept., 1842; served in the revenue department as assistant collector and magistrate, and also held the posts of Malayan translator to government, secretary to the college and university boards, and deputy and acting registrar to the Sudder court; director of public instruction, March, 1855; chief secretary to government and additional member of council for making laws and regulations, Oct., 1862; member of Madras Council, Oct., 1867, to Oct., 1872; temporary governor of Madras in the absence of Lord Napier, Feb.—May, 1872; retired from the Madras civil service, Oct., 1874; member of governor-general's council, June, 1875, to 1880, and acted as president of the council, Nov., 1878, and March, 1879; vice-chancellor of the university of Madras, Oct., 1871, and of the university of Calcutta, Oct., 1878; counsellor of the Empress of India, Jan., 1877; appointed Member of the Council of India, 1st Nov., 1887; is the author of various publications on educational and other subjects in connection with India, among which are 'Reports of selected cases determined in the Court of Foudjari Adawlut,' 1851; 'A compilation of papers relating to public instruction in Madras,' 1855; 'Memoir of Sir Thomas Munro,' 1881; &c.

ARBUTHNOT, SIR CHARLES GEORGE, Lieut.-General, K.C.B., R.A.—1st commission, Royal Artillery, June, 1843; served in the Crimean campaign, 1855, and was present at the siege and fall of Sevastopol (medal and clasp, brevet of major, 5th class Medjidie, Turkish medal); deputy adjutant-general, R.A., in Bengal, March, 1873, to Sept., 1877; acted as inspector-general of ordnance, Oct., 1877, to Sept., 1878; inspector-general of artillery in India, Sept., 1878, to July, 1880, during which period he served as brigadier-general in the operations in Afghanistan (medal, K.C.B.); deputy adjutant-general, R.A.; with the headquarters of the army in England, Sept., 1880, to Aug., 1883; inspector-general of artillery in England, Sept., 1883, to April, 1885; president, ordnance committee, May, 1885, to Jan., 1886; commander-in-chief, and member of the governor's council, Bombay, 16th Feb., to Dec., 1886; commander-in-chief, and member of council, Madras, Dec., 1886, to March, 1891.

ARBUTHNOTT, JOHN CAMPBELL, Bengal C.S. (Assam).—Educated at Fettes College,

Edinburgh; appointed to the Bengal civil service after open competitive examination of 1877; arrived in India, 21st Dec., 1879; assistant magistrate and collector, Bhagalpur, Dec., 1879; transferred to Assam, Feb., 1883, and served as assistant commissioner; deputy commissioner, Nov., 1890.

ARDAGH, JOHN CHARLES, C.B., C.I.E., Col., R.E., F.R.G.S., Private Secretary to Governor-General of India.—Educated at Trinity College, Dublin; entered royal engineers as lieutenant, 1st April, 1859; captain, Aug., 1872; major, Nov., 1878; lieutenant-colonel, Nov., 1882; colonel, Nov., 1887; attached to the special embassy during the congress at Berlin, June, 1878, and was made a C.B. (civil), July, 1887; appointed assistant to Her Majesty's commissioner for the delimitation of the frontier of Bulgaria, Sept., 1878; appointed commissioner for the demarcation of the Greek frontier, June, 1881; was employed during the conference at Berlin on the Greek frontier in 1880, and during the conference at Constantinople on the same subject in 1881; served in the Egyptian campaign of 1882-4, including the operations at Alexandria, and the battle of Tel-el-Kebir (medal with clasp, bronze star, brevet of lieutenant-colonel and 4th class of Osmanieh); served in the Soudan in 1884, commanding royal engineers and chief of the intelligence department; battles of Teb and Tamai; 2 clasps, C.B. (military), May, 1884; served in Soudan expedition, 1884-5—Nile; commandant of Base at Cairo, and in the expedition of 1885-6 with the frontier field force; has received the 3rd class of the Medjidieh; appointed private secretary to the Right Hon. the Marquess of Lansdowne, governor-general of India, Dec., 1888; C.I.E., Jan., 1892.

ARGYLL (DUKE OF), HIS GRACE GEORGE DOUGLAS CAMPBELL, K.T.—Born in 1823; succeeded to the dukedom in 1847; took an active part in the controversy in the Presbyterian Church of Scotland relating to patronage, and published various pamphlets and essays in connection therewith, 1842-1848; elected chancellor of the University of St. Andrew's, 1851; lord privy seal, 1852-1855; postmaster general, 1855; lord privy seal, 1859; in 1860 he was again appointed postmaster general, and subsequently lord privy seal; secretary of state for India, Dec., 1868, to Feb., 1874; lord privy seal, May, 1880; resigned in April, 1881; elected rector of the university of Glasgow, Nov., 1854; presided over the 25th annual meeting of the British Association, held at Glasgow, Sept., 1855; president of the Royal Society of Edinburgh, 1861; is hereditary master of the Queen's household in Scotland, a trustee of the British Museum, and hereditary sheriff and lord lieutenant of Argyllshire; published the 'Reign of Law' in 1866; 'Primeval Man' 1869; a small work on the history and antiquities of Iona, 1870; 'The Patronage Act of 1874,' 1874; observations 'On the important question involved in the relation of Landlord and Tenant,' 1877; and in 1879, 'The Eastern Question from the Treaty of Paris, to the Treaty of Berlin, and to the second Afghan War.'

ARMSTRONG, FRANCIS B., Financial Department, India Office.—Educated at Cheltenham Proprietary College, and King Edward's School, Birmingham; junior clerk, financial department,

India office, 30th March, 1869; senior clerk, Sept., 1883.

ARMSTRONG, JOHN ARTHUR, Lieut.-Col., R.E., late Public Works Department, Punjab.—1st commission, June, 1860; joined the public works department, Nov. 1865, and served in the Punjab as assistant engineer; served in the Hazara Campaign, 1868, as assistant engineer in charge of field telegraph; executive engineer, Sept., 1869; assistant to chief engineer, and assistant secretary to government, April, 1877; served in Kashmir on special road work, 1881-82; superintending engineer, 3rd class, March, 1886; retired, March, 1887.

ARMSTRONG, JOHN SCOBELL, Bengal C.S.—Educated at Marlborough College; appointed to the Bengal civil service after open competition of 1864; arrived, 3rd March, 1866; served in the revenue and judicial departments in Bengal; deputy collector of customs, Calcutta, Dec., 1877; magistrate and collector, 2nd grade, and collector of customs, Calcutta, April, 1883; port commissioner, Calcutta, Nov., 1887.

ARNOLD, GEORGE, late Store Department, India Office.—Clerk in storekeeper's department, Royal Arsenal, Woolwich, Oct., 1855, to Dec., 1858; sub-inspector of stores, India store depot, Dec., 1858; supervisor, 1st grade, March, 1877; retired, 1886.

ARNOTT, JAMES, Surgeon-Lieut.-Colonel, M.D., C.M., Medical Department, Bombay.—Joined the Indian medical service, 1st April, 1867; arrived in India, 25th Oct., 1867, and was employed under the military department till Aug., 1868, including service in the Abyssinian Expedition (medal); and subsequently as assistant-surgeon in the David Sassoon Hospital, Poona, and in the European General Hospital, Bombay, and as professor of pathology in the Grant Medical College; also acted as civil surgeon, and registrar of marriages, Poona, and in medical charge of the Deccan and civil engineering colleges, and as superintendent of the lunatic asylum and jail; acting surgeon, Gokuldas Tejpal Hospital, Bombay, Jan., 1878; also acted as presidency surgeon and marine surgeon, Bombay; surgeon, European general hospital, Bombay, March-April, 1882; professor of midwifery, Grant Medical College, and obstetric physician, Jamsetji Jijibhoy Hospital from April, 1882; physician, European General Hospital, May, 1891.

ARNOTTI, NAPOLEON, Major, R.E., Military Works Department, Government of India.—1st commission, 22nd Dec., 1865; arrived in India 13th Dec., 1868, and served in Bengal and the Punjab on irrigation duty; executive engineer, Jan., 1879; officiated as secretary to agent to governor general, Baluchistan, and as secretary to defence committee in 1882; and employed on special defence works, Aug., 1887; superintending engineer, Dec., 1889; deputy director general, military works, Sept., 1890.

ARTHUR, OSWALD GEORGE, Bengal C.S. (N.W. Provinces).—Educated at Winchester and Christ Church, Oxford; appointed after open competitive examination of 1888; arrived, 8th Nov., 1890, and served in the N.W. Provinces as assistant magistrate and collector.

ARTHUR, SIGISMUND RAYNOR, Bombay C.S.—Educated at Charterhouse, and New College, Oxford; appointed to the Bombay Civil Service after open competition of 1886; arrived

in India, 19 Nov., 1888; assistant collector and magistrate, May, 1889.

ARUNDEL, ARUNDEL TAGG, B.A., Madras C.S.—Educated at University College, London; appointed to the Madras civil service after open competitive examination of 1864; arrived, 18th Nov., 1865; served in various districts of Madras from Aug., 1866, as assistant collector and magistrate, sub-collector, and acting collector and magistrate; private secretary to governor of Madras, July, 1875; acting deputy director, revenue settlement, April, 1878; president of the municipal commission, Madras, June, 1881; fellow of the Madras University, March, 1882; principal assistant to collector and magistrate and agent, Vizagapatam, Oct., 1884; district and sessions judge, Kistna, Dec., 1884; collector and magistrate, Kistna, Dec., 1885.

ASHBURNER, LIONEL R., C.S.I., late Bombay C.S.—Appointed to the Bombay civil service from Haileybury College, 1848; served in the revenue and judicial departments in Broach, Surat, and Kaira; on the outbreak of the mutiny in 1857, was appointed superintendent of police, Kaira, and organised a body of horse and foot for the protection of the eastern frontier; special commissioner for trial of rebels, 1858; collector and magistrate, Kandeish, 1861; revenue and police commissioner, N. division, 1872; member of council, Bombay, Aug., 1877; retired, 1883.

ASHTON, G., Correspondence Department, India Office.—Clerk, 2nd class, 7th April, 1884; attached to financial department.

ASHUTOSH GUPTA, M.A., Bengal C.S.—Appointed on probation under the native civil service rules, March, 1885; confirmed, July, 1885; has officiated as magistrate and collector.

ASTON, HENRY FAURE, Barrister-at-Law, Bombay C.S.—Educated at the South African College; appointed to the Bombay civil service after open competitive examination of 1867; arrived in India, 9th Oct., 1869, and served as assistant collector and magistrate, acting political assistant in Káthiawár (April, 1871, to June, 1874), acting judge and sessions judge, railway magistrate and cantonment magistrate; on famine duty, Sholapur, Sept., 1877; acting under secretary to government of Bombay in the political, judicial, and educational departments, and secretary to the council for making laws and regulations, Dec., 1881, to March, 1883; 1st grade assistant judge and sessions judge, Aug., 1883; judicial assistant to the political agent, Káthiawár, March, 1885; 3rd grade judge and sessions judge, Nov., 1886; 2nd grade, June, 1891; on special duty in connection with settlement cases, Baroda, April, 1888.

ATA-ULLAH KHAN, MIRZA, Sardar Bahadur, Hon. Lieut.-Colonel.—British agent at Kabul, June, 1895; retired, June, 1891.

ATKINS, JOHN DE COURCY, Bombay C.S.—Educated at University College School, London University, and Balliol College, Oxford; appointed to the Bombay civil service after open competitive examination of 1878; arrived in India, 9th Nov., 1880, and served as assistant collector and magistrate, and forest settlement officer; acted as under-secretary to government, Bombay, revenue, financial, &c., departments, in 1886, and again in 1889 and 1890; acting postmaster-general, Bombay, April, 1891.

ATKINSON, FREDERIC JAMES, Financial Department, Government of India.—Joined the service as probationer, 8th July, 1873, and served in Bengal as district grain accountant and on audit duty and as officiating accountant-general; served also as assistant accountant-general, Bombay, Bengal, and N.W. Provinces; assistant comptroller, Burma, December, 1888; officiating comptroller and commissioner of paper currency in 1890; deputy accountant general, N.W. Provinces and Oudh, June, 1891.

ATKINSON, JOHN NATHANIEL, Madras C.S.—Educated at Marlborough College; appointed to the Madras civil service after open competitive examination of 1876; arrived in India, 3rd Dec., 1878, and served in Madras as assistant collector and magistrate, acting sub-collector and joint magistrate, and assistant sessions judge; on special duty under the commissioner of salt revenue, April, 1885; acting deputy commissioner of salt revenue, Oct. to Dec., 1885; head assistant to collector and magistrate, Sep., 1886; sub-collector and magistrate, Jan., 1891.

ATLAY, Venerable B. T., M.A., late Ecclesiastical Department, Bengal.—Arrived in India, 17th Oct., 1867; chaplain of St. Paul's Cathedral and of the general hospital, Dec., 1867; senior chaplain, Oct., 1877; chaplain of Naini Tal, Feb., 1879; senior chaplain, St. Paul's Cathedral, and chaplain of the general hospital and presidency jail, Jan., 1882; commissary to the Lord Bishop, and officiating chaplain in charge of the diocese, March, 1883; archdeacon of Calcutta, Sept., 1883; retired 1889, and was appointed vicar of Willesden.

ATUL CHUNDER DUTT, Madras C.S.—Educated at the Free Church Institution, Calcutta, and at Christ's College, Cambridge; appointed to the Madras Civil Service after open competition of 1887; arrived in Madras, 14th Jan., 1890, and served in the revenue survey department, and as assistant to collector and magistrate.

AUCOTT, ELLIOTT, Funds Department, India Office.—Clerk, 2nd class, 27th Feb., 1886.

AULAD HUSSAIN, KHAN BAHADUR, C.I.E., Central Provinces Commission.—Joined the service, 29th July, 1844; served in various subordinate posts till Dec., 1864, when he was appointed extra-assistant commissioner; on special duty at the Delhi assemblage, Dec., 1876; C.I.E., Jan., 1882; acting small cause court judge, Jubbulpore, July, 1883; assistant commissioner 1st class, Sept., 1883; settlement officer, 2nd grade, Raipur, Jan., 1885.

AUSTIN, WARE PLUMPTRE, Madras C.S.—Educated at St. Paul's School, and Gloucester School; appointed to the Madras civil service after open competitive examination of 1862; arrived 28th Dec., 1864; served in the Madras revenue and judicial department from May, 1866, as assistant magistrate and collector, sub-collector and joint magistrate, and acting collector, magistrate, and political agent; district and sessions judge, Oct., 1884, and again in 1888; collector and magistrate, Nov., 1886; acting collector of sea customs, and of the Madras district, protector of emigrants, superintendent of stamps and stationery, and chairman of the harbour trust board, Jan., 1891.

AYLING, WILLIAM BOCK, Madras C.S.—Educated at Weymouth College and Magdalene

College, Cambridge; appointed to the Madras Civil Service after open competition of 1886; arrived in Madras 30th Jan., 1889, and served in the revenue survey department, and as assistant to collector and magistrate.

BADCOCK, FRANCIS WILLIAM, Bengal C.S.—Educated at Harrow School; appointed to the Bengal civil service after open competitive examination of 1867; arrived in India, 24th Nov., 1869; served in the revenue and judicial department, Bengal, as assistant-magistrate, joint-magistrate, and deputy collector, and officiating district and sessions judge, and was placed on special duty, as settlement officer, Bhagulpore, Monghyr, and Purneah, July, 1873, and also in the office of the board of revenue, Calcutta, Jan., 1879; officiating inspector-general of registration, March, 1879; joint magistrate and deputy collector, 1st grade, Feb., 1883; district and sessions judge, 2nd grade, Jan. 1888.

BADEN, EDMUND, Auditor's Department, India Office.—Writer, 4th Jan., 1859; senior inspector, Sept., 1860.

BADEN-POWELL, BADEN HENRY, C.I.E., late Bengal C.S. (Punjab).—Educated at St. Paul's school; appointed to the Bengal civil service after open competitive examination of 1860; arrived in India, 31st Oct., 1861; assistant commissioner, May, 1862; officiating postmaster-general, Punjab and Scinde, Aug., 1865; from July, 1866, to April, 1869, served as small cause court judge, Lahore, and subsequently in the forest department as officiating conservator and officiating inspector-general, and was placed on special duty, Nov., 1872, and April, 1876; officiating commissioner and superintendent, Umballa, April, 1880; on special duty in Madras, Oct., 1880, to Oct., 1881; additional commissioner, Lahore and Rawulpindi, Oct., 1881; deputy commissioner, 1st class, Dec., 1882; officiating judge, chief court, Punjab, May, 1883, and again April, 1884, and Jan., 1887; divisional judge, 2nd grade, Nov., 1884; divisional judge, 1st grade, Sept. 1886, but to continue to act as judge of chief court; confirmed in latter appointment March, 1880; retired, May, 1889, but employed on special duty under Government of India, revenue and agricultural department; has published 'Punjab Products,' 'Punjab Manufactures,' 'Manual of the Land Revenue System and Land Tenures of British India,' and 'Manual of Jurisprudence for Forest Officers.'

BADGLEY, WILLIAM FRANCIS, Colonel, I.S.C., late Survey of India.—First commission, April, 1857; arrived in India, May, 1857; served during the mutiny in Central India and Oudh, and afterwards with the Lushai expedition in charge of a survey party (medals and clasp); appointed to the Topographical Survey, May, 1865, and was employed in Rewah and Bundelcund, and in the Khasi, Garo, and Naga Hills; deputy superintendent, 3rd grade, Dec., 1880; in charge of Guzerat survey, March, 1884; officiating assistant surveyor-general in charge of photographic and lithographic offices, March, 1886; retired, June, 1889.

BADOCK, HENRY W., Accounts Department, India Office.—Junior clerk, 11th Feb., 1876.

BAGNELL, HENRY WILLIAM JAMES, Bombay C.S.—Educated at Eastbourne College, Keble College, Oxford, and Trinity College, Dublin;

appointed to the Bombay civil service after open competitive examination of 1880; arrived in India 1st Nov., 1882; assistant collector and magistrate, Jan., 1883; forest settlement officer, Aug., 1887; 2nd assistant collector, May, 1891.

BAGSHAW, CECIL, Forest Department, North-Western Provinces.—Joined the service as temporary sub-assistant conservator, 26th Oct., 1869; assistant conservator, 14th Aug., 1871; deputy conservator, Oct., 1876; employed in the military department in the Kuram Valley, Aug., 1879, and at the Forest school, Dehra Dun, Dec., 1879; conservator, 3rd grade, Jan., 1889; conservator of the school circle and director of the Forest school, March, 1890.

BAILEY, FREDERICK, Lieut.-Colonel, R.E., Forest Department, North-Western Provinces.—Arrived in India as a lieutenant, R.E., 12th Nov., 1864; served under the military department till July, 1867, when he was appointed assistant engineer, 1st grade, P.W. department; executive engineer, 3rd grade, June, 1871; from Aug., 1871, served in the forest department as deputy conservator and superintendent of forest surveys; conservator of forests, and director of Indian forest school, in addition to his duties as superintendent of forest surveys, Sept., 1878; promoted to 2nd grade of conservators, April, 1883.

BAILLIE, DUNCAN COLVIN, Bengal C.S. (North-Western Provinces).—Educated at Inverness Academy, Glasgow Academy, and Edinburgh University; appointed to the Bengal civil service after open competitive examination of 1877; arrived in India, 10th Nov., 1879, and served in the N.W. Provinces and Oudh as assistant magistrate and collector, and assistant commissioner; assistant settlement officer, 1st grade, April, 1888; officiating collector and magistrate, Sept., 1889; joint magistrate, 2nd grade, Sept., 1889; 1st grade, May, 1891; provincial superintendent of census operations, April, 1890.

BAINBRIDGE, GEORGE, Brigade-Surgeon-Lieut.-Colonel, M.D., M.R.C.P., M.R.C.S., L.S.A., Bombay Medical Department.—Joined the service 1st April, 1867; arrived in India, 25th Oct., 1867; civil surgeon and superintendent of jail, Khandesh, Feb., 1871; acted as deputy sanitary commissioner; also as ophthalmic surgeon and professor of ophthalmic medicine and surgery and of comparative anatomy, Grant Medical College, May, 1878; surgeon-major, April, 1879; acting Presidency surgeon, Aug., 1882; acting senior surgeon of Sir Jamsetji Jijibhoi Hospital, and professor of surgery, Grant Medical College, Feb., 1885.

BAINES, JERVOISE ATHELSTONE, Bombay C.S.—Educated at Rugby, and Trinity College, Cambridge; appointed to the Bombay civil service after open competitive examination of 1868; arrived in India, 12th Nov., 1870, and served as assistant collector and magistrate, assistant political agent, acting educational inspector, and cantonment and railway magistrate; on special duty at Bombay as compiler of general administration report, Sept., 1878, and again Sept., 1879; acting under secretary to government revenue, financial, and general departments, March, 1880; deputy superintendent of census operations in Bombay Presidency, and under secretary to government, general department,

census, April, 1880; 2nd assistant collector and forest settlement officer, Dec., 1882; on special duty to supervise the work under the Income Tax Act in the Bombay Presidency, May, 1886; also acting as director of agriculture from Dec. 1886; 1st assistant collector and magistrate, Oct.-Nov., 1886; acted as junior collector and magistrate, 1887, 1888, and 1889; on special duty, June, 1888; served under the Government of India as census commissioner from Aug., 1889.

BAIRD, ANDREW WILSON, Lieut.-Col., R.E., F.R.S., A.I.C.E., Mint Department, Calcutta.—Lieutenant, R.E., Dec., 1861; arrived in India, March, 1863; served from May, 1864, in the Bombay Public Works Department as assistant engineer, and was employed on the harbour defences and reclamations; executive engineer, Oct., 1867; served in the Abyssinian Expedition as assistant field engineer, 1868 (medal); from Dec., 1868, served in the Trigonometrical Survey as assistant surveyor, and was employed in Kattywar and Guzerat; assistant superintendent 1st grade, Oct., 1871; in July, 1877, was appointed to the general superintendent and control of tidal observations upon the Indian coasts; on deputation as commissioner, Venice Geographical Congress and Exhibition, Aug., 1881; deputy superintendent, July, 1882; officiating master of the mint, Calcutta, in 1885 and 1886; 3rd grade deputy superintendent, March, 1886; mint master, Calcutta, Aug., 1889; is the author of various contributions to the Bombay Gazetteer, and of papers and reports on tidal observations, including a paper read before the British Association, 1876, and an account of the spirit-levelling operations of the great Trigonometrical Survey of India, read before the British Association in 1885; joint author of the annual tide tables for Indian ports.
